lines.x: Our tale begins in a modest teahouse, its building hardly tall enough for the two floors it's split into.

The interior is simply furnished, the space neither cramped nor bare. Ten wooden tables, meticulously laid out, seat six each. The tea stains have been scrubbed as much as they'll permit. It all speaks to a diligent owner.
The teahouse's server casts ladles of water across the floor, as he mops each and every tile to a mirror sheen.
It seems a while since this teahouse last welcomed a guest. One wonders what sort of customer could warrant such a conscientious welcome.
And with perfect timing, they come in.
One, two... A party of two? Welcome, this way. Would you like a pot of tea to start?
We're fine. First things first—is this Mangshan Town?
It is Mangshan Town.
Are you the shopkeep of this place, or the server?
I am the server.
Keep not in?
The keep is not in.
It's like I'm hearing myself echo. At least you give answers.
One more question then—did a stranger from outside come by today?
They did.
When? What did they look like?
Right now. Two people, both bandit-like.
Wang ba dan, don't you screw around with us!
The boor of a man's temper flares, and he throws a hefty palm across the server's face that sends him crashing to the floor.
But the server does not anger in kind. He calmly picks himself up, and stands up again, like none of that just happened.
What strange guests you are. No tea, no wine? It's beyond me to serve you, then.
Kid, be nice. He just works here.
Check the kitchen in the back. And close every door shut while you're in there.
Take it easy. Of course we want tea.
But we're waiting for someone first. We need to have our tea with her.
And in short order, the proclaimed plus-one arrives.
Scarcely has the girl stepped inside to look around when the tip of a crossbow bolt nestles against her back, and the point of a machete to her throat.
Haha...
Look at that, it's the Lu brothers. Who knew I'd bump into you here?
I think you wouldn't have come if you did know.
Still, good to see you. Now stick around.
Server boy. Shut the door, if you don't mind. And hang up the closed sign.
Then, we'll take a pot of tea.
Here's your tea. Take your time.
The 'Three Blades of Wind and Cloud' treating me to tea? All the heartlands know you. I feel like a VIP now.
Shame it's just the two of us now.
A few days ago, you framed our trio with that secret tome. That sicced three whole gangs and the Yumen garrison on our asses. A hundred men, maybe more, all hunting us down.
I made it, he made it. Our youngest brother died outside Baizao.
So... what could we do but invite you for a cup of tea?

Haha... Absolutely, we should say cheers to you jianghu heroes for making it. Here, may I toast you two with my tea, in place of wine? And then, after meeting on such good terms, we can part on them too?
You need to shut the—!
Let's cut the chit-chat—do you still have the secret tome?
So you caught wind of the deal? Is that why you were waiting for me here?
That's right.
And what happened to my guy, Mr. Pu? Don't tell me you killed him?
Guess.
*sigh*... And there's nothing I can say to convince you that you don't want this tome?
You already screwed us over once. If we don't get this tome today, then the wrong you stuck on us, and the death of our third brother, will both go unanswered.
Alright, now I'm curious. Do you fine heroes really know how this tome here came to be?
Of course.
Rumor has it the Grandmaster of Yumen, who perfected the martial arts, wrote all his learnings in a tome before wading out of the jianghu.
And not long ago, that tome finally fell into the commoners' hands, though almost no one can actually say they've seen it.
We brothers did some asking around too. All signs pointed to you having the tome.
So presumably, you two want the tome so you can master the ultimate kung fu? Claim First Under Heaven?
What I'm asking is, what will this do for you in the long run? Will you rule the jianghu with an iron fist? Will this avenge your dead brother?
Or are you going to duel to the death once you realize there can only be one First Under Heaven?
You're pushing your luck—bring it out.
He says, making a subtle show of his machete again.
To which the girl can only shake her head and sigh. She pulls out a small parcel from her travel bag. Its rectangular dimensions are those of a book, no doubt.
Fine, fine. It's not like I needed the tome anyway, I was just gonna sell it to—well, anyone.
So, fine gentlemen, what's your bid? Because as a note, the last buyer I had lined up set the bar high.
She says, gesturing a '7' in the air.
I have a better offer.
Your life.
*sigh*... Sir, there's no reasoning with you, if you do business like this.
As I see it, it's already a very fair offer.
If my info's right, the Grandmaster's tome was meant for the general leading the Yumen guards.
I don't know how you got your hands on it, but it can't have been legal.
We're bandits, fine lady, and you're a crook. We do us and you do you. Justice and injustice are both above our paygrade.
Whoof, you're no fun... Alright, alright, I really screwed the hound on this one this time. You've got the stuff, can I go now?
Not yet.
I may not be a delicate man, but I know a trouble magnet when I see it. If I let you blab about this to the entire jianghu, I fear we won't live to train as the tome instructs.
Well, if you can cram it a little, and get a little smarter too... Who knows?
Save some jibber-jabber for your next life.
And if there is a next life, remember this: Play stupid games, win stupid prizes.
What the...?
The man can hardly process the sight of his machete dropping to the ground.
His unwavering grip has never betrayed him throughout his grueling years in the jianghu.
Yet now he can't even hold his own blade.
*pant*... *pant*... Sh... shit... Bro... I think...
Someone... here... poisoned us...!
I poisoned you.
Producing a hemp rope from out of nowhere, the server silently busies himself. In a flash, the three incapacitated guests are bound up like zongzi on the plate, and deposited neatly in the corner.
Figures I'd mistake you for just a teahouse server, sir. My eyes must be going bad.
Poisoning us so finely, you must be a jianghu old-timer too? Tell us your name. If we're dying, at least we should go out knowing.
You weren't mistaken, I'm just a teahouse server, no jianghu old-timer. My friends call me 'Hu Jun'.
I imagine you know Mr. Pu too, then?
He's one of those very friends.
Heh...
One more thing, if you don't mind. This tome... why do you want it?
To leave this town.
It holds five hundred and twenty families, and going around visiting each one takes two thousand and nine hundred steps.
For years upon years, neither number has changed at all. I want to get away from here.
But if I want to leave, there needs to be some change. And if I want some change, I need this secret tome.
I don't understand what you're getting at, sir...
No matter. It's not as if all the realm was made for human minds to comprehend.
Yeah, fair.
We've fallen to you, I admit it! Kill us or whatever, just do what you like.
Whether or not you admit you've fallen to me doesn't change anything.
Sir, you've gotten what you came for. Why bother taking anyone's life?
Who the hell are you? When did you seat yourself?
If you'd have my name, it's Yun Qingping. I was just passing through on my travels.
I've been sitting here since noon, actually. You were just so fixated on the secret tome, I naturally didn't enter your sight.
Catching sight of the elusive boy, Hu Jun grips his dagger tight, but following a moment of thought, he lets go again.
I have no reason to fear you. I 'treated' all the water in this teahouse. You should be poisoned too.
You wouldn't need to fear me anyway, sir. I'm just a scholar who couldn't even truss a featherfowl. There's nothing I could do to you in a fight.
But you insist on saving these people?
Yes. I ask for you to look down on them with kindness, and not to kill again.
These people are not good people.
If he'd gotten the tome just now, he would've silenced everyone else all the same—he even said so himself, just now.
They may be utter scoundrels, but surely your heart is still a good one, sir. So why sully your own innocence for them?
Just hand them to the authorities, and justice will take care of them as a matter of course. As for the secret tome, I'll swear to keep my mouth shut.
How about it, sir?
The way you talk reminds me of my friend. He's a good person. You must be too.
But for now, I cannot trust you.
I won't kill you, but I will ask you to step forth, so I can tie you up.
*sigh*...
You didn't want trouble, and yet trouble's come knocking.
No noise. Or else all four of you are dead.
(Cooperative nod)
Excuse me, is this teahouse closed for the day?

lines.st: Dear Auntie Qingyan,
I hope this letter finds you well.
I heard about your resignation. I don't know of the specifics, but I admire you deeply for your courageous act. No wonder Mother always exhorts me to follow your example.
To make a long story short, I have something urgent I want to entrust to you in this letter.
An old friend of mine, called Yun Qingping, came with me to Baizao. We've been due to return and report back to the Sui Regulator. But three days ago, he went missing.
My friend is a very careful person. Going AWOL like this is unusual to say the least. I fear for the worst.
Based on the clues I've found, he was last headed out of the city via the north edge, in the direction of Mt. Mang, same as your return trip to the Tianshi Bureau.
I was going to search for him myself, but the Sui Regulator has been inundated lately, not to mention whispers of another incident in Father's army. I'm all but stuck here.
You're the only one who can help me find my friend's whereabouts now, Auntie. I've enclosed a photo of him with this letter. If you find him, I beg you contact me ASAP.
Yours in due deference, Zuo Le
Blessings:
May your health be sound, and your vacation merry.
P.S. Please show up at our next reunion dinner, Auntie. I'm sure Father and Mother are eager for the story surrounding your resignation.

lines.x: That morning...
The nerve of this boy. Now he's sending me on missions...
...'Courageous act', what nonsense...
No respect for elders...
Before she knows it, Lin Qingyan has set foot into this strange town.
It's a rustic one, low buildings littered about. Few wander the streets. The scene here is a still and peaceful one.
Did Mt. Mang always have a town like this at its foot?
This is odd. I lost signal as soon as I entered. I didn't think the Tianshi Bureau had missed any nearby towns with its signal towers...
Young lady, young lady—
Hm?
Young lady—I've never seen you before. Are you from outside? How rare, how very rare.
Correct... Are outside visitors infrequent here?
They used to be. Lately there's been more.
I'm not surprised. The entrance to this town is very remote, and not marked on any maps to boot...
Sorry, do you happen to know the way to the nearest Tianshi Bureau?
Tianshi Bureau, Tianshi Bureau—can't say I've heard, can't say I've heard.
Can you point me to the road out of town, then? I didn't take full note when entering, and I lost the way I came by the time I thought to look back.
Out of town, out of town—I don't know, I've never been out.
You don't know?
Slow down, slow down—have a pancake, take your time, look around. Everything is done when it's done. No rushing it.
I'm assuming you live here? How long have you lived here for?
Me, me—yes I do. I've been here ever since a pastry lit the first fire in my belly.
Lit a fire? In your belly...?
*ahem*... Thank you, I'll keep looking.
Young lady, young lady—pancakes, fresh from the oven. Have one.
No thanks, I'm not hungry... Did you just dig that out from your pocket? That pancake is oven-fresh?
It's done now, well-done now.
Ouch! This is scalding! You were holding this to your chest—?
No rushing it, no rushing it. Go back to looking, young lady, until you're heated through just right.
Excuse me, wait!

Eight thousand nine hundred and ninety-one. Eight thousand nine hundred and ninety-two. Eight-thousand...
Ah.
Careful! Are you hurt?
Gaze fixed on the ground, a young woman walks headfirst into Lin Qingyan and promptly tumbles straight to the ground, where something falls out of her clothes.
Lin Qingyan immediately checks her over, then turns to look back, only to find the old man with the pancakes is nowhere to be seen.
Umbrella, my umbrella... Mister told me not to let go of my umbrella...
Here. And this letter. It fell out of your sleeve.
The girl hurriedly takes her umbrella, then with difficulty stuffs the letter back in her sleeve.
Lin Qingyan looks to the clear blue sky.
Try and stand up, young miss. Let me help you.
(This girl's so light...)
How far did I count just now?
Er, eight thousand nine hundred and ninety-three?
Eight thousand nine hundred and ninety-four. Eight thousand nine hundred and ninety-five...
Young miss, are you alright? Do you need me to take you home?
Home? I can't go home, I promised to meet him at the tofu stall—
Ah... oh no, he told me it was a secret, to protect it with my life, not to tell anyone even if they beat me to death!
Did you hear that? Did you hear about my secret meeting with him at the tofu stall?
That's your personal business. Let's say I didn't hear.
Thank goodness, I'd rather not get beaten to death—where did I count up to?
Eight thousand nine hundred and ninety six? What are you counting?
He told me to count to nine thousand when I go to meet him, and once I do—oh no, I said it again! I'm going to get beaten to death!
If I'm not missing something, I think 'beat you to death' is just a brand of Yanese hyperbole...
Unless you're telling me, Miss, you believe your life is in danger—
Eight thousand nine hundred and ninety-seven, eight thousand nine hundred and ninety eight...
Er...
Eight thousand nine hundred and ninety-nine, nine thousand! It's time! I'm off to see him—
There's... something strikingly odd about the people in this town...
...What's this? Is this... It's the letter that fell out of her sleeve.
'CLASSIFIED SCOOP, NOT FOR PRYING EYES'?
Wait, Miss!
Hu Jun, Hu Jun—I'm here to see you!
Shh, quiet. This way.
Hu Jun... Why did I have to count to nine thousand before seeing you? My lips are chapped from counting out loud now.
I had no other choice. That's the only way you know to keep track of time.
Did anyone see you come here?
A bunch of people, but none of them know I was coming to see you!
You get the stuff?
Yep, in Mister's cabinet, second drawer. It said 'CLASSIFIED SCOOP, NOT FOR PRYING EYES' on it.
Perfect. Give it to me.
A-Alright...
Oh no, I can't... I can't find the letter! I must have lost it on the way!
But that's alright, I read what was inside. It said 'Three quarters past the Hour of Wei, deal at the old teahouse'.
You sneaked a look at the contents, and you lost the letter itself.
What's wrong, Hu Jun? You're crinkling your forehead up so hard. It makes you look terrifying. I don't like it.
You're sure that's what it said? The time? Three quarters past the Hour of Wei?
Yeah! I can't actually tell the time, but my memory's really good.
Hu Jun, what's wrong? Say something. Do I have to count to nine thousand again before you stop ignoring me? Okay, one, two, three, four, five...
No, stop that.
Hu Jun, Hu Jun—I really don't like you being this way. Your face looks so scary.
Xiaoshu, I trust you dearly. If everything goes smoothly, I was planning to get out of here with you. We could leave together.
But you're not ready yet. You haven't learned to keep secrets. That's why I can't—
What are you doing?
You can't hide it from me. Your eyes are wet, Hu Jun. You're hurting a lot, aren't you?
Mister says, when things get wet, I shouldn't jump straight to wiping them, but I can help by blowing on them. Are your eyes a little drier now?
Ah, you held my hand too tight just now! Now there's wrinkles in it.
Goodbye, Xiaoshu.
This is the direction she went, I know it. How did she lose me so fast?
She said she was meeting up at the tofu stall. That should be right—ah, there.
Far across the alley, Lin Qingyan spots the umbrella the girl was sporting.
She hurries over, intending to return the lost letter, but as she draws near it becomes obvious—
The umbrella lies forlorn on the ground, the girl unaccounted for.
Welcome, this way. Sit wherever you like.
I ask because I saw the sign hung up outside. Are you closed or not?
If a guest comes for tea, then we can hardly keep closed.
Would you like a pot of tea to start?
Duoxie.
The scent of the green tea is positively pungent. Lin Qingyan reaches a hand, but stops.
You're the server of this teahouse? Do you have time for a few questions?
Please, ask away. I'll do all I can to answer.
I see this teahouse is situated in the most bustling part of town. Why close for the evening in the middle of the afternoon?
We play it by ear, you see. Sometimes, nobody wants a drink of tea.
But what if a customer was coming here for something other than tea?
Sometimes, our guests need a shelter from the rain and a spell to rest. They're welcome inside to sit for a while, yes.
Drink tea, or don't. But if you don't take tea and you don't plan on leaving quietly, then the problems begin.
That's fair enough... You make me wonder. Did you get any troublesome customers like that today, before closing up shop?
My guests today all drank tea.
Last question...
It just caught my eye, honestly, but there's what looks like a spot of paint stuck to your lapel.
You're a teahouse waiter, not a studio painter. Where would you get paint like that on your clothes from?
And one more thing. I happened to see this color of paint today, on an umbrella.
You look awfully anxious. What's the matter?
I mean, look at the time... Didn't you close it ages ago? Your deal, I mean, three quarters past the Hour of Wei?
Brain working in overtime, Hu Jun promptly flips the table up, cutting off Lin Qingyan's line of sight to him. It's just long enough for him to scramble like mad to the upper floor.
Halt—!
H... help...
You.
You have the secret tome... So he tricked me...
Give it to me.
You asked for it, then.
Thank you, Deputy Minister Lin... I never imagined I'd run into you here.
How do you know me—wait, hold on, you're... Yun Qingping?
I've heard much about you from Zuo. It's an honor to finally meet—
But according to Zuo Le, you're missing?
Missing? Did Zuo not see the letter I left him? I really feel like I should've foreseen that...
Set that aside for now. What's going on here?
The three beside me got into a fight, I think, over some kind of treasure, and then—
Three? What do you mean three?
Lin Qingyan glances over again. There's two bandits bound up like fowl, but all that lies next to them is a pile of severed hemp rope.
Well, that's strange...
Curses—!
Lin Qingyan hurries out into the street, where a good few townsfolk are milling around to see what the commotion is.
She hears the single sharp strike. She sees someone crash through the upper floor window and fall to the road hard, right in front of the teahouse's entranceway.
She fully expects to see someone injured. Possibly even a corpse. But all that greets her eyes...
Is a pile of shattered teapot shards.

lines.st: To quote... Once written word and painted scene fall to the masses to critique, all time itself might well as end; once ware and lacquer bear appraisal by the street vendors, the injustice should damn us all...
But Hu, my potted brother, Hu, I'm no such common folk if I might say, and when, in all the days we drank together, did I fall victim to the wine? Tell me, how did I ever do you wrong?
Without you now, my drinking friend, where do I find someone to share a toast again...?
*sigh*...

lines.x: Mister, Mister! Bad news!
Hu Jun... Hu Jun's—dead!
Wha—
When did this happen... How could this...?
It was in that teahouse he always stays in! He was sent flying from the second floor, and fell to his death!
It must... It must've been that wicked woman who did it!
She'll—probably come for us too!
*sigh*... What do I always teach in this school?
Don't speak lightly of what you never saw. You didn't see it with your own eyes. How can you jump to conclusions?
But Mister, you also told us to deduce everything we can.
That wicked woman's been killing so many people! How could Hu Jun's killer be anyone except her?
'Deduce everything you can' is for logic. Not for happenings, and especially not for people.
You wouldn't want others to turn their malice on you. You should imagine others wouldn't want your malice turned on them either. That is reasonable.
But if a person errs, and you say thus they can do nothing but err, that is not reasonable.
But never mind that, now. We'd be talking a lot longer to cover all the ethics there. We have more pressing matters at hand.
Hu Jun fell from the teahouse and perished. Did anyone collect his remains?
I DEFINITELY saw that part with my own eyes!
After Hu Jun fell, a woman dressed like a Tianshi picked up his pieces and took them away.
What is a Tianshi doing here...?
This is no laughing matter. You're sure that's what you saw?
Absolutely definitely! The Tianshi was right there when Hu Jun fell.
And then, after that, I even saw the Tianshi going around asking people things while carrying his corpse.
What a rotten, rotten turn... I fear we're in a bind now that he was seen.
But as long as Hu Jun's remains are with that Tianshi, then above anything else, we need to find some way... some way to get him back.
You mean, you want us to steal Hu Jun's corpse from that Tianshi?
How many times have I told you? You must refer to the remains of our deceased as 'remains', not as corpses...
Also, say 'purloin', not 'steal'. I've taught you this too. If it's for your own selfish sake, it's 'stealing', but if it's—
No, never mind, we're short on time. That Tianshi will be here any moment. Do as I taught you, each of you. Go take your places...
Pardon me... you must be 'Mr. Pu'?
Lin Qingyan is silent as she enters the tiny schoolhouse, and stays silent as she proceeds to carefully scrutinize each and every object.
After enough time passes for an incense wick to burn out, the elderly teacher sat watching her finally can't help but ask.
Excuse me...
Allow me to introduce myself. I am Lin Qingyan, Judici... a Tianshi from the Tianshi Bureaus.
I guessed as much from your dress.
Do you often chat with Tianshis, Mr. Pu?
There's a Tianshi Bureau neighboring this town. The townsfolk's lives wouldn't be much without the care of your Tianshis. Hence why I recognize.
And, er, Tianshi Lin... what exactly brings you here today?
This may sound funny, but something odd has happened on my very first day here in Mangshan Town.
There's a teahouse in the south with a server named Hu Jun. At three quarters past the Hour of Wei this afternoon, he poisoned four customers and held them hostage.
I just so happened to witness it, and right as I was going to apprehend him, I saw him plummet from the second floor with my own eyes.
I ran over to see where he'd landed, and all I found was a shattered teapot.
I thought I must've been seeing things. But the fact of the matter is, multiple other people saw what I saw.
So you mean, someone fell from upstairs, died... and turned into a broken teapot? Now that's one for the history annals...
Erm... Tianshi Lin, would you let me have a look at this teapot?
Lin Qingyan reaches into her bag, brings out a wrapped bundle, and sets it on the table.
Mr. Pu is moving to take it when Lin Qingyan suddenly places a hand on it.
One moment please, sir. I'd like you to answer a few questions first.
I... of course, Tianshi, ask away.
While making my inquires about the deceased, a lot of townsfolk said they knew of a Hu Jun, and that you and this Hu Jun were apparently well-acquainted.
What plagues me as odd is I reside in Baizao most of the time, just a stone's throw away from the foot of Mt. Mang, yet I've never heard anyone mention Mangshan Town.
And there's not a single official tending to the town. Everyone resolves their disputes here instead, at your 'School'.
It sounds to me like you hold quite the sway here?
Nonsense... nonsense...
Tianshi Lin, I can answer all your questions now...

This town used to be lively, in the old days. But time marched on, and Baizao kept on building itself bigger and bigger, until all our youth ran off to the city to find work, and left our town population to dwindle.

We still hang onto the 'town' title, but fact is, we're only five hundred or so households. Factor in the nearby Tianshi Bureau, and we never do get disturbances. Hence why the imperial court left us be.

As for myself, I read a book or two in younger times, that's all. I was a penniless scholar without enough knowledge to pass any entrance exam, so all I could do was stay here, teach the children to read, and scrape by.
So it's been for years. The folks call me Mister like I'm a wise man. They come to me when they run into trouble or want a penny for my thoughts. Makes me feel like a house of cards.
I think you're too humble, sir. Bringing up the youth is a virtuous thing. You have every right to be proud of yourself.
But something I've noticed, sir. Your collection in here, the books, calligraphy, paintings, antiques, curios, stationery... None of these things are just your average ware.
Well...
Ah, no, you're mistaken, Tianshi Lin. These are presents from my Tianshi acquaintances. Odds and ends from all over, mixed up in one place. Just a little old, nothing valuable per se.
I see...
And the Hu Jun you mentioned... *cough* *cough*—
Watch out!
For some mysterious reason, the candlestick suddenly falls over on the table, almost splashing its scalding wax on Lin Qingyan's hand.
She bolts up straight, only for her chair to flip about too, narrowly avoiding tripping her.
Seizing this single moment, Mr. Pu swaps the bundle with uncanny swiftness.
What am I seeing...?
Tianshi... a-are you alright...?
Everything in here's far too old. Most of it's wonky in some way or other. Forgive me.
No matter.
Now where were we...?
Oh, the Hu Jun boy, yes. I can tell you he's a friend of mine.
He's the waiter at the old teahouse. Decent at brewing tea. You could call him a drinking companion of mine, be it tea or wine.
Do you know if he had any enemies?
No, I've no clue... The Hu Jun I know is a down-to-earth person...
But one person talks to another and the dish bumps the bowl and, well, maybe he said something that riled someone up without realizing, or owed someone money for a few days too long...
Who can go through life without making a few 'enemies', mm? No, I can't help you there at all...
Is that all you're willing to tell me, sir?
Well, it's not a question of willing... You see, this is all I kn—
Save it.
You're an example for us all. I don't want to coerce you into doing anything that'll ruin that. Think twice before you speak, that's all I ask.
When you're willing to converse with me, sir, we'll meet again.
Pardon me for tonight. I'll excuse myself now.
Mister, Mister, is the Tianshi gone? Is she leaving us alone?
Yes...
So did you get Hu Jun's remains?
After a moment, Mr. Pu warily withdraws the bundle from under the table, and opens it with care.
Inside is not a single shard of teapot. Only a pile of pebbles.
This Tianshi Lin... She's a smart one. And a sincere one...
So be it... I'll have to make another quick run.
You look after the place. I'll be back in a flash.
Mister, Mister—can we help with anything?
Very well. Come.
It's the dead of night, and Lin Qingyan walks through the empty streets. If anyone is out at this hour, they'll see something most curious.
Behind the Tianshi, at a distance of ten steps, a handbroom leans on the wall. And for reasons unknown, wherever she goes, the broom stays behind her by that exact distance.

But never mind why a domestic handbroom is languishing outside; there's a single embroidered shoe on the roof too, pitter-pattering as it follows the broom, and then a calligraphed character on the wall, following that shoe.
And behind that, a jumping shoulder pole, a rolling medicine bottle, a clattering rattle drum... and at the very back of this bewildering procession, who should it be but Mr. Pu.
They all stop outside the door to an inn.
Mr. Pu, the Tianshi's gone...
And she went into this inn?
I can confirm that, but we couldn't find her when we looked inside.
Hmm...
Mister, Mister—
Did you see the Tianshi?
No, no!
I saw that wicked woman! She came after us!
What—
Found you.
Ai-yo... my back...
It's you...
Oh, I never wanted to believe it... but who else could it have been, other than you?
That's enough. The only thing I want to know is, why did you trick me?
Trick you? When did I ever trick you...?
You always said the secret tome was a fake. You kept telling us to ignore it. So why did that teapot get involved in the deal at the teahouse, and why would he get this tome?
You've got it wrong... you've got it all wrong...
I don't care who got what wrong. There's one thing from you, and that's it.
Tell me what the deal is with the 'personification' technique in this tome.
Are you serious...? Is that how badly you want to leave this town?
Surely you know I have an old friend to meet?
All these years, I've been begging you to tell me how to leave this town, but you kept on urging me, 'just be patient'.
Well, the tome's right here, right now. Any more excuses, 'Mister'?
You've taught so many so much. Would it hurt to just teach me one more thing?
Do the people you've laid low in the fight for this tome mean anything to you?
You want to become a human, but you forewent learning human kindness and love. You jumped straight to human resentment and ruthlessness.
It will only hurt you if I let you leave this town...
Love... Hmph. None of you would know a thing about the love in my heart...
Time's up! Enough hiding things!
I'm sorry... but I'm just as clueless as you about this 'personification' technique.
Hate me if you must. Kill me if you like.
Asking me to darken my conscience, and lie to my students, though... never.
Mr. Pu, I have always respected you.
So don't blame me. You left me no choice.
Before the swordswoman's sword can fall, a blade flies in from outside, sending her skidding back with the might of a thunderclap.
Astutely, she realizes her foe's power is beyond the pale, and without a second thought, she vaults through the window and flees.
She ran...
Tianshi Lin... Did you hear all that?
Most of it. And I'd guessed most of it.
*sigh*...
I can believe you have troubles, sir, but I think you'll agree your actions prior to now have not been proper.
No objections, Tianshi Lin... Oh, I've made a fool of myself with this, a fool of myself...
But how did you know I was being held captive by...?
A talking broom showed me the way, while asking that I save you.
I don't know if anything can surprise me today.
Sit down, Tianshi Lin, have some tea.
And as he says it, a plate drags a teapot and teacup over, and tea is served.
Lin Qingyan just furrows her eyebrows at the sight.
These items...
They're the students of this school. And all of them are geists.
That explains the strangeness of the townsfolk I met today...
So is everyone in this town...?
Some are humans, some are geists. As for how many of each, I'm not sure myself, not anymore after all these years.
The town became this way a long, long time ago. None of what I told you earlier was a lie per se, Tianshi.
I've heard tell of possessed beings born out of Feranmut influence. But I've never seen a 'geist' with intellect approaching that of humankind.
Have you forgotten, Tianshi Lin, what rests nearby?
...The Tomb of Sui.
Correct.

We all know about the power of Feranmut incarnations to possess objects, and Sui was all too powerful. Even suppressed by the town for a millennium, the mere might that leeched out of the cracks of the earth sufficed to turn some things geistly.
Ordinary people should never know about any of that. Sir, are you...?
A Feranmutologist at the Sui Regulator. Decades ago, at that.

Life ends somewhere, and so does knowledge... My Feranmut studies were achingly incomplete, and the research had wicked away my own humanity. I handed in my resignation and left to live a secluded life in the mountains. That's when I found this town.
Decades ago, this town saw frequent possession incidents, which the Tianshi Bureau would send a Tianshi to quell if it felt like it.
But I had to feel sorry for geistkind, born into our secular realm not knowing up from down. And how steeped in humanity some were, their consciousness not so far from a human's.
Why simply watch them run amok and be crushed? Nothing stopped me from trying to teach them human rites and values. Maybe they'd even be able to coexist?
So little by little, this school came to be.
How noble of you.
No, no... I can't be prizing those words from a Tianshi's lips.

It's ridiculous on the face of it. I spend one half of my life on studying the 'bestial', and the other half on teaching the 'geistly' to be human, only to look back and realize I've half-baked both. What do I really know...?
Still, since sequestering away, I've come to find the little things oh so endearing.
I had thought this was how it'd be until my time was up.
But see, a few days ago, I received a letter.
A letter...?
'CLASSIFIED SCOOP, NOT FOR PRYING EYES'?
Someone claims to have gotten hold of a one-of-a-kind secret tome, penned personally by that Grandmaster of Yumen.
Rumor has it that Grandmaster is a Feranmut incarnate, but has the capacity to form himself a human's body. So analogously, the tome's teachings can allow geists to 'become human'.
Even Yumen is involved in this? That explains the incident Zuo Le mentioned.
Wait. Can this tome really make the 'geistly' human?
To be honest, I have no idea if that's true or not. But importantly, quite a number of geists believe it. And it's caused a great deal of unrest.
Are you suggesting the letter's writer aimed to sow unease in this town?
*sigh*... Hard to say, you know, hard to say...
All that I know, I've told you now. What are your plans, Tianshi Lin?
Matters of the bestial Sui are out of my job's scope. But as a Tianshi of the Tianshi Bureaus, geists hurting people is something I cannot overlook.
If whoever's orchestrating this has malevolence in mind, all the more reason to put my foot down.
That's good... I could tell you were the upstanding sort. I'm at ease knowing this investigation is in your hands.
Tianshi Lin... just one more thing.
Hu Jun's 'remains'. Could you hand them to me...?
Certainly...
Are you giving him a burial, sir?
A burial, yes, but it's not just a burial.
This study we're in... is the Hall of Morn and Eve. Every broken item that rests in here, once was Geist.
A geist does not live and die as a human does.
They come alive from humans' attachments to things; they perish when their vessel breaks or the attachment is rid.
If the broken vessel can be mended, perhaps one day, the spirit can manifest again.
So this teapot—no, Hu Jun here...?
Hu Jun's owner was a tea farmer here. He loved tea, and knew it through and through.

He used to clear a few mu of land in the mountains to plant tea. Every April before Qingming, when he picked and fried his leaves, he'd go around town ladling out wellwater as he pleased, and all of it filled with the fragrance of tea.
But he wasn't content. He devoted himself to finding the best tea under heaven, and the clearest spring water.
One year, he heard of the mothertree of Mount T'ien tea, by the southernmost border of Yan. With nary a goodbye, he hired a ship fleet and went south.
But once he was gone, he never came back.
A speck of that tea farmer's infatuation dwelt within this teapot, as it accompanied him day in and day out. Who can blame our Hu Jun, for wanting to leave the village so...
Was that tea farmer... a friend of yours?
From her bag, Lin Qingyan brings out a small box. Safekept within, there rest a teapot's shards.
My thanks, Tianshi...
Mr. Yun, I've been looking forward to meeting you.
I'm so, so sorry for keeping you. There were some mishaps along the way.
You said you wanted to tour this town first, yes? Has it satisfied you?
Of course! The people, and the whole character of the place, have really opened my eyes.
I apologize you had to wait for so long. How about we get going?
Right. Come with me.
Oh, wow... Now that's a pretty stone.
Ooh, it's got such a beautiful pattern, and it's really smooth. If I carve it a bit, I might be able to sell it at the market for a good price...
Say, my young friend, could you give that stone back to me?
Back to you? I found this stone in the river, what do you mean, back to you?
It's a stone I wanted to add to my garden that took me quite a lot of effort to find. I accidentally left it here as I was returning back.
If you give it back to me, then when my garden is restored, you can play there. How does that sound?
No, no, no. It's yours just because you said it's yours? I saw the stone here, and I picked it up first. That means it's mine.
*sigh* Such an unreasonable little rascal...
Alright, then how about this? I'll trade you some other treasure for that stone.
Name your price. Anything for the stone.
Really...? Then I want a hundred, no, a thousand...?
Forget hundreds or thousands. Millions, billions, enough fame and fortune to sustain you until you're old and gray will be yours, just like that.
You...
Do we have a deal?
No, no, no, the answer's still no.
People are always trying to take advantage. If you're okay with paying that much for this stone, it means it must be worth way more than that.
So I'll take real good care of it. I want to see what makes this stone so special.
What a clever little one...
Very well, I'm in no hurry.
When you change your mind, I'll come find you to buy that stone.
Ridiculous... Absolutely ridiculous!
The old man's making trouble even on his deathbed!
Don't say that, brother. Dad's condition is getting worse by the day, and soon...
We haven't had the time to be the most filial children. He only has the one final wish, and we should do our best to make him happy.
Our sister's words are much kinder than yours, little brother.
Filial piety is the greatest of virtues. You might be rich now, but do not forget your roots.
Yes, yes, yes, the two of you speak so highly of filial piety, but who was it who actually took care of him when he became ill?
Calling in everyone because of a lost stone. If I went to Baizao to report it, the Judicial Review Office would have me locked up for obstructing official business. What am I supposed to do, huh?
Besides... Have we ever had such a stone in our house before?
Who knows? As the second young master of this family, surely you are the most aware of how many properties we own?
Me? Pah! Stop trying to act all high and mighty.
You've never come home once these past few years, then the moment Dad got ill, you came running back like a dutiful son. Who do you think you're trying to fool?
Alright, enough arguing. If you can't help, then don't cause trouble, okay?
That Tianshi has only just arrived. Let's wait for her to find that stone for us.
Master, this is a Tianshi from the Tianshi Bureaus, and a former deputy minister at the Judicial Review Office.
She is here to help you find that stone. Are you willing to tell her about what happened to that treasure of yours?
...Hmph...
There's barely any devotion in those children of mine...
Tell them that, if my stone is not found, they will not be seeing a single fen of my money.
Whomsoever finds that stone for me, I shall bestow upon them half my estates.
So you are the venerable Mr. Wan.
What sort of stone is it that you lost?
It is a stone I plucked from the river when I was young, one unique across all of Terra.
What I meant was, what sort of treasure was this stone? Was it jade, or some other precious mineral?
Neither... It was merely an ordinary stone.
Then do you know who would desire this stone?
I do... but the one who desires it would never steal it...
Who is it? And how do you know—
Qingyan has many more questions she wishes to ask, but the old man on the bed turns his head away, unwilling to speak another word.
Where was this stone normally kept? When was the last time the residents saw it, and at what hour?
It is normally within a small box the master had specially made, which was hidden in the deepest storeroom in the house.
The evening before last, I was cleaning that storeroom, and everything was as it should. The next morning, I found the box smashed against the ground, and the stone inside gone...
Can I trouble you to show me where the stone was lost?
Come, come, please, come in! Today, I have prepared some meager wine, so as to offer you my deepest gratitude. If you are unsatisfied, please, I beg your forgiveness!
Congratulations on your new home, good sir! May your fortunes and profits be grand! Here, a fowlbeast from the family coop, along with two baskets of eggs—
We're from the same clan and generation, cousin. We might not have been close in the past, but why be strangers now? Please, call me Brother Wan.
Alright then... Brother Wan! Then take these as well. Shoes my mother worked on through the night. Please, don't mind how they look.

She says she knows how much you've rushed about doing business these past few years, crossing hill and stream. The boots you buy in town might be sturdy, but they can't beat the comfort of homemade shoes.

Once I've settled down, I will definitely go to visit Auntie. When my home was washed away by a deluge when I was young, she trekked several hundred li to give me two sacks of millet, despite her bad back... I never forgot.

*sigh* All you had left was a single stone. The adults thought you didn't have long to live, but nobody could've expected you were tougher than rock! You're really something, making it through all that!
You made a living with your own two hands, enduring a life of hardship without complaint. You crawled your way out of the backwater, started up a business, gotten married... You've got my respect!
It's all just luck, really...

But since I was forced into such a situation, not charging headlong into the future wasn't an option. I couldn't just lay down and die, could I? I'm truly lucky to have grasped my life back. I can hardly give up now.
The youth holds his hand to his chest, his fingers clenched tight around the stone hanging at his neck.
Now please, sit! If we keep chatting, the braised hock will all be gone!
No rush, everyone, there are plenty of seats for all—Heaven above, is it really you?!
Yes, it's me. I've come again.
It's been quite a while. What're you here for today? Surely not to try and buy this stone again?
You're on the mark, I'm afraid.
Since you've already settled down, started both a family and a business... Are you willing to give me that stone now?
Naturally, the answer is... still no!
Putting aside the fact that this stone has saved my life, just wearing it to bed lets me sleep easy.
It's my lucky charm, so how could I give it to you? It simply will not do. Just give up already.
Very well then.
Huh? You're actually giving up?
No, of course not.
I still dearly adore that stone, but I cannot seize that which another loves.
I shall wait once more, wait until you wish to put it down, at which time I shall take it away.
This is the storeroom where valuable assets are kept?
Yes, the master kept his important account books and deeds here. Not just anyone is allowed in.
Did anyone enter on the night the stone was lost?
Unfortunately, there was a torrential downpour that night. If I remember correctly, I never saw a light turn on in this storeroom, nor did I hear any sound coming from it.
Who normally has access to this room?
There are altogether four copies of the key to the room and the cabinets. The master's three children each have a copy, while I keep the master's copy in his stead.
But the stone's box has its own key. The master keeps the only copy on his person at all times.
And the box was kept on top of that standing cabinet?

The master normally does not allow others to touch the stone. After becoming bedridden a few years ago, he has not had the time to fiddle with it either, so it has been placed somewhere out of the way.
Account books, deeds... Someone who knows what these are worth...
If a thief came in from outside, would they have sneaked over here? This residence has many more valuables that would have caught their eye.
Pardon, did you say something?
Nothing, just thinking about a case I was recently looking into, but I doubt it has any relevance to this.
Right, there's something I'd like to ask. Master Wan's three children, what sort of people are they?
Well...
No matter what may have happened, I am merely a butler. It would be inappropriate for me to discuss the young masters' and young mistress's affairs...
Please, be at ease. I am not asking you to judge them as people, but rather simply asking what their day-to-day work is, and under what circumstances they come to visit Master Wan. That is all.
This is for the sake of your master's final wish. You may speak freely.
*sigh*...
When it comes to the master's three children, if you were to only look at their achievements, they would all show great promise.
The elder young master, oldest of them all, is a scholar. He has always boasted impressive grades, and currently holds a post at the Academy.
The second child, the younger young master, took after his father, and has recently brought much custom to the family businesses.
The youngest, the young mistress, is uninterested in such mundanity, dedicating herself to music. She is a musician renowned far and wide.
Based on your tone, it sounds like they have their hidden flaws?
I dare not say, I dare not... Who would care for the opinion of a lowly servant such as I?
But... from my bystander's point of view, these three children are not particularly close to the master.
The three of them moved out when they came of age, rarely returning even during festivities...
Does Master Wan have a bad relationship with his children?
*sigh* Another long story, I'm afraid.
When the master was but a shepherd boy, he had nothing. It was only after much bitter toil that he was able to start the family businesses.
But when it came to the care of his children, he found himself willing but unable.
It isn't hard to imagine.
Tianshi Lin, surely you do not suspect...?
I simply understand the situation a little better.
By the way, why does your master value this stone so? Did he plan to sell it?
That has never been the case, as far as I know.
That stone is the master's most precious treasure. We staff normally never have the opportunity to see it.
I once suggested taking it to the jade store to be appraised, but he scolded me with a great fury in return. I have never dared mention it again ever since.
I do remember one thing, though. The master once mentioned a strange individual thinking of buying the stone, one who has been thinking for several decades now.
Several decades...?
Your master mentioned finding this stone in the river several decades ago...
Does this place still exist?
I'll be off now, master...
Right. Do I still owe you any wages? If so, I'll think of a way to—
It's all settled. The beams and pillars that survived the fire were made of top-rate cedarwood. The coffinmaker's already gathered them up. The money that's left over... I put it on the stove.
Then... Then you can go now. And there's no need to call me 'master' anymore.
Brother Wan... You've done lots of good deeds, enough that nobody would think you any less virtuous than anyone else. How did it all go so wrong...?
I didn't do all that just to cultivate virtue and show off.
Who would've thought your good name would make you a target for vicious robbers! Damn it all, how can Heaven be so blind?! Letting such evil men go free just makes it harder for us honest folk!
Alright, alright... That's enough of that.
I'm halfway to being old and gray, my family and funds scattered to the four winds. Being able to hold on to my life and... this stone... means I'm still rather lucky.
The middle-aged man holds a wooden box tight to his chest. The blood slicking his hair hides spots of gray. He is no longer young.
The 'house' he painstakingly built, brick by brick, tile by tile, all by himself for half his life, is now no more than himself and the stone.
Have I come at a bad time?
I'm afraid I can't offer you hospitality this time around.
Fate is a cruel, cruel mistress...
Here for the stone again?
At first, yes, but...
How do you feel now? Do you believe it to be an ordinary stone?
I believe...
It's a totally ordinary stone, and I'm a totally ordinary person. A gust of wind and some drizzle is all it takes to bury us in mud.
I've met many people before, but very few of them were like you, dragging yourself out of abject poverty through sheer stubbornness and grit to achieve greatness.
Even fewer can... No, nearly nobody your age can crawl back up after losing everything in a flash.
Are you saying I should just resign myself to fate and abandon it all? Including the stone?
No... I simply wished to ask you one thing: Have you given up?
Of course not!
I've crawled out of the gutter once, and I can do it again... I must crawl out!
The middle-aged man seems to have set his mind on something. He lifts up the box, and speaks to the kindly man solemnly.
You always wanted this stone, didn't you? I'll give it to you, but I'm not abandoning it. I'll come to redeem it one day.
You mean this is to be collateral?
That's right. With it, I'll borrow some money from you.
I'll plant saplings where the trees were burnt down and lay new bricks where the walls collapsed. Right, I might be getting old, but I'm not dead yet!
It's just a fresh start. I've done it all once, and I can do it all again...
But this time, I'll do it even better!
Hmm, I understand. But... Ah, never mind.
Hah, don't tell me you'll only accept it if I give it to you for nothing?
I wouldn't accept it if you did.
What I want is a totally ordinary stone. You haven't let go of your obsession with it, so this stone would be too heavy for me.
The kindly man puts down the basket on his back and sifts through it. From amongst many artifacts of porcelain and jade, he pulls out a fowlbeast crafted of pure gold.
From that golden fowlbeast, he plucks many feathers, also made of purest gold. They are heavy, and clink together with a crisp melody.
Here.
Why are you helping me?
I am accustomed to the road here. On whichever day I feel like seeing if the stone is still there or not, I need only walk two paces to come over.
But if you were to fall into ruin and wander off with that stone, I would not know where to look for it. And that would be rather troublesome for me, don't you think?
According to the elderly gentleman yesterday, this should be the area where he found the stone.
The river has become a graveyard... Even the trees that only sprouted after the deluge seem decades old.
There's someone here...?
Hey, I told you you should've come with us.

Look at me, I became a mandarin a long time ago. It doesn't matter if it's flowers and trees or vases and pots; in Garden of Grotesqueries, when they see me, they have to bow with respect and call me—
Wh-What do they call you?
'The Young Minister of Rains!' Heh, I told you, where it rains and where it doesn't rain in Garden of Grotesqueries is all up to me.
I simply need to jump in a pond and—Pow! There shall be rain!
Sounds really impressive.
What about you? What ministry are you with? What do others call you?
Nothi... W-Wait! They call me... 'Great Hero'!
Great Hero? What ministry is that under? How come I've never heard of you?
The Great Hero is... is a big wooden box. When I'm inside it, nobody can open it!

Th-That does sound rather impressive... But I'm a Young Minister, so why are you a 'Great' Hero? That's not right! When you come with me to the Garden of Grotesqueries, you shall be called 'Young Hero'!
Oh, okay...

Look at you, did you just wake up? I'll tell you something. Once, we were both in a basket carried on someone's back. The basket's master was carrying us through the river, when you suddenly fell out of the basket—
Tell me, what were you thinking then?
I... I don't really remember.

Then do you at least remember how you 'woke up'? All of our siblings who were brought back to the Garden of Grotesqueries were awakened by the master. Who knew there'd be someone outside the Garden powerful enough to make even willful stones bow their heads!
What do you mean, powerful... He's just a stinky rock! And he's stupid and mean too!
On rainy days when you're supposed to rest, he runs out to the fields. When other workers have gone home already, he goes to the river to dig up rocks while his tummy's rumbling.
He said he wants to build a new house! He's got no money to get people to lay the foundations, so he's doing it himself with a shovel. He can't afford bricks, so he makes his own out of yellow clay.
It took him seven or eight years to build that home by himself, but he still wasn't done. As soon as it was finished, he was out the door to go do business.
What an idiot! Stupid rock!
He worked so hard on that business to get results, but once he got robbed, he had nothing left!
*sigh* So he did all that hard work for nothing?
He could have settled down and lived out the rest of his years earning an honest living. But he just wouldn't give up, and put himself through all that torture again—
But he's finished now that he's sick... His children are unfilial, and the family and friends who visit just want his stuff.
What really makes me mad is that right now, he's still blaming himself for not looking after the box! He still thinks he lost the stone!
It sounds like... you're a bit angry at him, but you also pity him a little? If that's the case, why did you run out here instead of staying with him?
Because I... I don't really know... Why does he never know when to give up?
I heard from some friends from outside the Garden of Grotesqueries that things like people fall into holes really easily and are unable to climb out their entire lives.
If that's the case, then come with me to the Garden of Grotesqueries. I'll introduce you to my brothers and sisters. They all know loads of stuff, so they can definitely help you figure things out!
Garden of Grotesqueries... What kind of place is it? Is it fun there...?
Of course it's fun! You definitely won't regret coming with me! I'm telling you, that place has—
This is bad, someone's here.
Wait a moment—!
Lin Qingyan reveals herself, stepping towards the big tree. The two young voices cut off abruptly.
There is nothing behind the tree, let alone a child. She looks under the bushes hidden in the tree's shadow, finding a smooth stone lying there, as quiet as a rock.
Transformed by obsession, as if become an object possessed...
What sort of obsession has ingrained itself into this stone, I wonder...
Have you called us here because you have found the stone, Tianshi?
Indeed.
Not only have I found the stone, but I have also identified the robber who stole into your manse.
Please don't keep us in suspense, then. Tell us what happened, quick.
There is no need to rush. I require a cooperative partner in deduction to explain how this stone was lost, and what connection the robber has to it.
If I may trouble you to stand here.
The man is reluctant, but ultimately acquiesces to Lin's command, standing next to the towering cabinet.
Now, use your key to open this cabinet's lock.
What sort of trickery are you up to...?
Cooperate with the Tianshi, brother.
Don't dawdle. Just do as she says so we can all be at ease.
...Hmph.
In the end, the man continues to follow her command, taking a key from his pocket to unlock the cabinet.
Now what?
Yes, such an act would not be difficult under normal circumstances...
But I fear it would be hard to be so nimble in working the lock if it were a dark and stormy night. A few knocks and smacks are to be expected.
Qingyan gives the cabinet a light push. A box atop it falls, smacking the man on the head before it clatters against the floor.
The box smashes open, and from it rolls a sparkling, smooth stone. She steps forward, picking it up before it rolls out of the storeroom.
In truth, the robber was not after the stone, but rather Master Wan's legacy, locked within this cabinet.
And in knowing where this house's true treasures were stored, they could no longer hide their avarice—
You... Get to the point already!
(Whispers) I am no mandarin of the Judicial Review Office, young master. I have no intention of getting involved in your family affairs. There is simply one thing I must remind you of.

(Whispers) Under the Laws of Yan, the crime of theft applies equally between parents and children. Where unauthorized alteration of wills involves assets exceeding statutory thresholds, it shall constitute a felony.
(Whispers) I ask that you act with dignity.
You... You...!
Young masters... Young mistress...
Master Wan, he—
Return this stone to him, posthaste.
You and I both see true. It really is a good stone. It never abandoned you.
The old man opens and closes his lips, as if he has much he wishes to say, but ultimately, only a single sentence arises:
It's you.
Yes, it's me again.
That same smile hangs upon his face, the smile he wore ever since the very first time they met, when the old man was but a little boy.
The young boy plucked the stone from the river, lifting it in front of the passing stranger. As sunlight passed through it, it glowed with a wondrous luster.
So many years... and you've visited quite a few times.
The last time we met, you could still carry a bench to the courtyard door to enjoy a cup of tea with me.
You've always wanted that stone. How many times have you come to ask for it, and how many times have I refused you...?
Why do you keep coming?
Because I really do adore that stone. It also happens to be just what my garden lacks.
There was never any special reason for it. The beauty of the finest crafts of heaven and earth has never been rational.
You are... a very strange man.
Every time I saw you off, I thought, surely he won't show up again, right?
But you always returned, whether it took ten days, two weeks, three years, or perhaps five. I never understood how you could be so carefree every time you left empty-handed.
Every time I descend from the mountains, I gain all that I see and hear, all that I think and ponder. Even if I do not have the stone, how could you say I left empty-handed?
All that you see and hear... So, what have you seen?
I have seen cool breezes push cirrus clouds, seen fine blossoms and young chicks, and also...
I have seen interesting men, and interesting things.
Whenever I walk home, I would think: Will he give me the stone next time?
Haha—*cough* *cough*! Keep dreaming! *cough*! You had to keep running over to my place, all because I never gave it to you.
You call me a dreamer, but are you not the same?
True... We're the same.
Once I die, you won't need to keep coming over here. Pity. When my time comes, this stone will fall into your hands.
Not necessarily.
A man's strength has its limits. Just like how I carried a basket of stones across a river back then. Despite the care I took, a stone still fell into the river, which was then found by you.
Who would have thought you would never let go of that find for the rest of your life.
Haha, ha—You knew long ago I would never let go! That's right, you knew long ago...
Yes. I knew it from the day you stood in the river.
So... what did you hope to gain by finding me?
Most times, I simply wished to see it. I adore it, so naturally I wanted a closer look.
There are many stones in this land.
But not many that are interesting. To chance upon it is destiny at work.
Thankfully... there aren't many people on this land as stubborn as you, either.
When it comes to stubbornness, you are many li ahead of me.
I'm afraid it's this stubbornness of yours that allows even willful stones to bow their heads.
A man has but... a single life. From that deluge... I found my life again, thanks to the stone... and to you.
I cannot... give up on this life of mine.
That's right. You did it.
Despite falling into deep ravines and striking great boulders, you have never once given up.
I saw it all.
This stone... I give to you. I give it to you. You...
Since you have already awakened it, allow it to choose.
Heheh... You... bothered me my entire life... for this stone... Now... I can finally be free...
Yes. Put it down. You can put it down now.
The boy stares blankly at the stone in his palm. As sunlight passes through it, it glows with a wondrous luster. The river gently flows past, wetting his pulled-up pants.
He does not see his friends he always plays by the river with, nor does he see the man who always has a kind smile on his face.
There is no wind, no trees, no sky... He does not sense a single thing other than the river.
Time has stopped at the very moment he dredged up that stone from its waters.
(Melancholy smile)
The boy opens his hand, allowing the stone to fall back into the river, not thinking about whether it will sink to the river's bed, or follow its flow to the ocean deep.
He closes his eyes, and listens to the sound of the thunder, the wind, and the deluge's roar.
When are you going to be finished, mister?
No rush, let me think a little longer.
But you've been thinking for half a day now. Last time I came by, you were thinking, and the time before that too. Were you tricked by someone?
I've been here lots of times now, and I've never actually seen anyone play weiqi with you.
You're not wrong. I'm just... thinking about how I should proceed.
You need to consider how your opponent will react after you place a stone, and all the changes that will arise after.
Sounds like a pain. Just thinking about it makes my head hurt... Can't you finish this game first, then ask the one you're playing with to teach you later?
It's just weiqi, nothing's going to happen if you lose.
But I cannot lose.
The one who arranged this game promised me that, if I win, he shall grant me a wish.
A wish? Really?
*sigh* Shame I don't get any of this strategic thinking. I think I'm more suited for physical stuff...
The sun's about to set, so I should get home. There's still a pile of junk I need to sort out.
Zaijian!
Oh, right, I just thought of something. We've seen each other lots of times now, but I still don't know your name, mister.
My surname is Lan, while my name is Ke. 'Lan' of the orchid flower, and 'Ke' of possibility.
You do remember my name, right, mister?
Luo Qin?
That's right!
I really gotta go now. Mother said we need to move out of the house for a bit. When I'm back, I'll definitely come to see you again.
Zaijian!
The sound of the boy's footsteps slowly fades away. Night falls upon the weiqi board. Sunlight falls upon the stones. Rain and snow wash the stone benches.
Lan Ke picks up a white stone. He nearly places it down, but retracts his hand. Half a day later, he finally throws the stone back into its bowl.
His tenth dead end. He keeps it clear in his memory.
Time for yet another round of calculation. This time, he must come up with some wondrous move or the other to triumph.
We have arrived, Mr. Yun.
Did you not say earlier that the one who awaited me was atop the mountain?
'Tis true, 'tis true. That person is atop the mountain, indeed, but my duty is merely to show you the path leading up to it.
There is only one path going up the mountain, but it is not hard to walk. The forest around it has been cleared, so you should not run into any wild beasts.
You need only head up. The rest has been arranged for you.
I suppose it would be rude if I inquire too deeply...
Then let us say our goodbyes here.
May we meet again, Mr. Yun.
You say that, but...
I don't think I actually see a path up the mountain here...
The ground is completely covered in leaves. I doubt anyone has come this way in a very long time.
Not even signs of any beast trails...
Perhaps I should find a local to ask for help.
Good day?
Good day, my lady. Is something the matter?
May I ask if you have seen my husband?
He is young, with hair of brown and a tail point of pure white. He is wearing a cloud-white tunic, trousers the color of earth, and leather boots.
I... don't think I've seen anyone who matches that description. My apologies.
Is that so? Pardon me for wasting your time.
Hold on, madam!
Do you happen to be from these parts? I wanted to ask if there was a path up the mountain nearby.
You wish to ascend? Then I fear you've gone the wrong way.
The regular path up the mountain is on the other side of town. This is the back of the mountain, which is rugged and steep. The townsfolk rarely ever come this way.
(The back of the mountain? I distinctly remember coming straight from the town's main road. How did I end up on the back?)
I see now. Duoxie, madam.
But I believe... since the gentleman guiding me told me specifically to come this way, he must have considered all this. I will continue trying.
Please, excuse me.
I shall accompany you up the mount, sir.
I'm sure there's no need—
The road up the mountain is dangerous. If we are together, we can look out for each other.
Besides, I have already made inquiries everywhere I could in town. The mountain is the last place I have yet to search. If my husband is still in the area, the only place he can be is here.
Then, I shall have to thank you again.
May I ask how I should call you?
...Bai Jin.
Still stuck on that board, Brother Lan?
I'm hardly in a hurry.
You've been at it for so long, I've learned how to play myself.
This situation on the board, isn't it a 'three jie cycle'? They call it a 'triple ko' in Higashi. Shouldn't it be a draw according to the rules?
A draw... A draw is unacceptable.
The rules were very clear. Only through a win will he grant my wish...
No, I need to find a way to victory...
What's this wish you want so bad that you need to win this game? Isn't there another way you can make it happen?
Another way... What other way...?
There is no other way for two as separate as yin and yang to meet again...
O miserable fate... From where do you flow?
Will this do, Jin'er?
It's beautiful! You really are skilled, my dear.
And here I was, thinking your ability to bake porcelain only applied to weiqi pieces.
Heheh, I can do a lot more than that. Next time, whatever you fancy, whatever you like, I'll be happy to bake it for you.
Once we are wed, I'll put this vase in the most conspicuous spot in the living room.

'Vase in the east, mirror in the west, enjoy lifelong tranquility and rest'... We're still missing a mirror, then. But if we place one, we won't have anywhere for the clock. Well, the bell in town rings in the morning and the night, it'll do.
In time, there shall be flowers in the vase, and you in the mirror's shine.
Oh, that's enough out of you. We don't have much fuel left, so head out to the back of the mountain to chop a few logs while it's still light out.
Alright, just sit tight, then. I'll be right back.
I will. Dinner should be about ready when you get back.
What the... What's going on...?
Where did these monsters come from?!
Jin'er———!
So there was... a path here... all along.
Do you... need a hand... Madam Bai?
You do not need to concern yourself about me, Mr. Yun.
I grew up amongst the mounts and fields. This is but a leisurely stroll to me.
You, on the other hand, look rather pale. Would you like to rest a moment?
I shall... take you up... on your offer.
Hoo... Hah...
Your dedication is much like my husband's.
He sounds like a very conscientious man.
He is.
He approaches everything with absolute focus. It is rare for anyone's urgings to have any effect.
He will stay in one place without food, water or moving until he is done. *sigh* He really does inspire both pity and frustration.
At least he listens when I speak, so it's always me who has to pull him away by the ear. That's how it's always been since we were little.
But there was a gentleman who played weiqi in town who said this temperament was suited for the game.
With a few years of study, he could become a master, perhaps even a grandmaster. If he does, he can leave town and head off to distant lands to look around.
Your husband left to study weiqi?
Yes. He has been studying for a very long time now.
He throws his all into his studies and barely listens to what anyone has to say. It's simply too much, I tell you.
Haha... I have also heard about the endless marvels of black against white upon the gridded board, and how captivating they can be.
The land is vast, and there are many things that can trap a man in a mire. Even a memory can leave one pacing back and forth for quite some time.
Your husband must have his reasons for his dedication.
Why exactly are you trying to argue his case?
I was simply moved by his tale.
Well... You said your husband disappeared, but when exactly was this disappearance?
He...
It was nearly nightfall, and he went out to the mountain to chop some firewood. I never heard from him after that.
That truly is worrying.
Let us set off then, Madam Bai.
Do you not wish to sit a little longer? Pardon me for saying so, but your body does not seem the strongest, nor do you look accustomed to steep mountain paths.
While I might not be able to perform martial arts, I practice breathing techniques every day.
The path is yet long, so I must be diligent.
I see an ancient pavilion further up. We can rest again once we arrive there.
The game's still stuck in a stalemate, huh... He's been thinking about his next move for ages now...
Just how long have you been working on this one move, Brother Lan?
I don't know.
My grandson's now as tall as I was back then.
He didn't believe me at all when I told him about you. 'Who would spend their entire life on a single lousy game?', he said.
*sigh*...
Do you still remember when exactly you got separated from Sister Bai?
I don't.
After I left town, I got lost and was disheartened. I wandered the mountain for several months before arriving here. It can't have been that long.
And what day was that?
There are some things I don't remember all that clearly.
Then do you know today's date?
No...
But that's not important. Let us speak of other things once I solve this deadlock.
*sigh*...
Oh, everyone in town's been so in a tizzy over some treasure or the other that I never expected someone to come over here.
Here to see the game as well, youngster?
We're just here to take a rest—
'We'? But it's just you.
Just me? The lady I was climbing the mountain with...
Strange... She was here just a moment ago...
Yun Qingping looks all around him, but there is no sight of the woman.
There is only a young man, sitting at a weiqi board in silence.
And this gentleman is...?
*sigh* He's trapped in that game. There's no getting him out of it.
If you know how to play, help him out a bit.
This game state...
A three jie cycle. A prolonged battle that can only end in a draw.
But he's dead set on victory, so in the end, he trapped himself here.
I can't think of a way to get him out...
(Observes the board)
(Contemplates)
H-Heaven above, it's trapped another.
One after another, *sigh*...
A refusal to draw, victory the only satisfactory option...
If one focuses only on the jie, can one truly demonstrate superiority on the battlefield?
This boy... He yet lives.
But with such grievous wounds, he will have to endure many hardships.
I can't practice kung fu with this body of mine, so what use is there in me recording all the many styles under Heaven?
I don't understand, Grandmaster...
Yun, have you ever questioned the Grandmaster's plans for you?

Of course I have. When I first took this job, my heart still harbored some resentment. I felt like making me watch everyone practice kung fu every day was like chaining me atop a stake while dangling meat before my eyes.
The scent hits you like a fist, your eyes go wide with greed, but you can never have even half a bite. I tormented myself with an endless barrage of complaints as I suffered in discontent.
You don't look like you're suffering now.
Even though my progress is only theoretical, it is enough to soothe me.
But more importantly, I believe I understand some of the reasoning behind it.
Have I told you about my recent dreams, Zuo?
No, might as well tell me now.

One night, I dreamed that I would return to the countryside someday, a nameless, obscure man. All those many martial moves and techniques, when used upon the fields, will let you save a considerable amount of strength in tilling and hoeing the land.

Another night, I was a passionate warrior, quick to kindness and vengeance, an imitator of the Grandmaster. Strike, and sunder the tides cleft. Retract, and leave no peace bereft. A legend born from a single punch, before dying after all my breath is spent.
They both sound really different from the Yun I know...
Obsession is a quagmire. It is natural for those trapped within to not understand.
After much deliberation, there is but only one answer...
First give, then observe, and finally, take.
The stone should go here.
Yun Qingping picks up a white stone, paying no heed to the massive hundred-point battle raging on the board as he nonchalantly places it on the other side of the board.
Black takes the opportunity to wipe out the majority of white stones atop the board, but then comes a stunning reversal, leaving white with a clear advantage.
Lan Ke watches in stunned silence as the countless stones, both black and white, disappear as if they were but mirages. All that is left is a clear board, and three men surrounding it.
The stalemate has been broken.
My word... To think there was a move like that!
Duoxie, my friend, duoxie! I couldn't come up with anything despite thinking for so long. I never expected you to break it open the moment you arrived. You have my admiration!

You flatter me. I don't actually know much about weiqi. I simply thought that, since all things on this land are connected, martial doctrines that espouse give and take could be applied to weiqi as well. I didn't think it would work out so well.
Your weiqi skills far exceed mine, but since you were so focused on winning the jie cycle, you overlooked the overall state of the board.
No matter how you put it, this game is won!
Is that you... Luo Qin?
I'm Luo Qin's grandson.

When he was still alive, he'd always talk about the strange man back home playing weiqi atop the mountain. I've visited you many times, thinking I'd have to pass this task on to my own grandson. Didn't think this man'd put a stop to it all, youngster.
That means I've spent...
Over a hundred years here. The town's changed too.
So I've been... separated from Jin'er... for all these years...
My dear.
Jin'er?!
Yes, it's me.
Long time no see... dear.
It's really you! That Xian wasn't lying to me! I really did meet you again!
Amazing... Truly incredible...
Let's go home right away! Mom and Dad are still waiting for us—
Jin'er...? Why won't you... say anything...?
I see... Hahaha.... So that's how it is...
I was already... long ago...
But it doesn't matter. I got to... see you again...
With the game ended, the Xian keeps his promise.
And so, Lan Ke is free of his regrets.
Madam Bai...
No, you are not the Madam Bai of this tale.
Yes. After seeing my love, what more have I to pine for?
I was but a stack of joss papers before her cenotaph, soaked in his tears and longing.
Her desires, my destiny, were bound together in this meeting with him.
With him gone, there is no reason for me to stay here.
I beg the two of you, fulfill my final request. Place my love's grave by her cenotaph, then burn 'me' as an offering.
We may not have been able to grow old together in this life, but I hope that in the next... we will meet again...
'Madam Bai' falls silent, then bows deeply. And in an instant—
There is only a stack of joss papers, fluttering in the wind.
Joss paper... So this particular Bai Jin was a geist?
*sigh*...
A hundred and twenty years ago, for reasons unknown, a horde of geists fell upon the town one night, wantonly lashing out at anyone and everyone. Truly a calamity.
My granddad was saved by the young man named Lan Ke.
He helped many townspeople escape with their lives that night... but he himself had to be saved from the flood of geists by Bai Jin.
As for her... She died that night.
Mr. Lan and Madam Bai were kind people...
I heard those geists are born from thoughts. Once those obsessions are gone, they go back to being regular objects.
The secret passed down my family for three generations has finally borne fruit, so why do my tears refuse to stop...
I'm heading down the mountain, youngster. I need to make sure Sister Bai's request gets done, and also tell my granddad about what happened.
How about you?
There's someone I'm meeting on the mountain. I need to get going.
This mountain's so lively nowadays. No idea what kind of bigshot asked you to climb up the back side to meet up.
Well, I'll be off then.
If, by any chance, we meet again, you're welcome at mine for a cup of tea.
Old Luo carefully packs up the things on the ground, putting them in his basket before tottering down the mountain.
Was it you who broke the stalemate?
May I inquire as to who is speaking?
A half-life who deals with menial tasks, a hard-working geist is all.
You are Da—Chongyue's record-keeping man, the one by his side.
I presume you are one of the Sui proxies, then.
Forgive me for inviting you in such a matter. Let us ascend. There is a guest at the peak awaiting you.
So I was 'invited'...?
Indeed. At first, it was for other matters... but who could have expected you to chance upon that game and break the deadlock with a flick of your wrist?
(Loudly) You were right again! You happy, you weiqi failure?!
It was pure happenstance. Did someone really predict that would happen?
When the second brother left this board here, he proclaimed that only a man with true intelligence could resolve it.
Now that I think about it, him arranging this board here... The closest to 'human' of us siblings is the eldest brother, and calling one of his disciples over... Hardly an odd move.
Let us climb.
Remember to take the board with you. The game is over, so it's yours now.
Very well...
I do not know what to call you.
Just the one character, Liang, 'Liang' of the roof beam.
Mr. Liang, you said that only the Grandmaster's students could break this deadlock just now, but the game already had a player before I came here.
You mean that Lan Ke?
He really did make a promise at the time, which kept Lan Ke playing.
It was a game where the more obsessed you were with the jie, the less able you were to resolve it. He knew full well that a weiqi player would be mesmerized by them, yet still allowed him to join...
It could be said that the one who arranged this game simply wished to play, or perhaps...
The one who uses weiqi as a means to meet, has a heart as stubborn as this eternal deadlock?
Ah, a fine way of looking at it. Yes, very fine indeed.
(Barely-controlled vibrating)
Please, go ahead and tell it to his face next time.
We have arrived.
This is the one who wished to meet you.
I suppose it is fate that determined we should meet again.
It is a pleasure to see you again, Mr. Yun.
It's already a new day, sir.
Yes, I know.
Did you get stood up by whoever you were planning to meet?
Perhaps she was delayed.
You can wait, but I'll be closing shop.
I'll leave, if I'm in your way.
But I'll still have to wait at the door... this is the place where we agreed to meet, after all.
Oh, that's not what I meant! You can stay at your table. We've known each other for quite a while, after all.
I was a humble waiter when we first met. Now I'm the keeper of this teahouse.
